text: If you've used Docker, you've probably typed something like this: docker run nginx.
You press enter, Docker pulls the image, and seconds later nginx is running. This works really well.
But what does 'run' actually do? The word is a little vague, so let's break it down.
When you execute docker run nginx, Docker does three things: it pulls the image, creates a container, and starts the process inside.
Public images like nginx live in a registry — a shared library of images. By default, Docker looks in Docker Hub.
All of this happens on your own machine — the host. Docker itself, and the terminal you're typing into, both run right there.
The first thing Docker does is check whether the image is already on your machine — a simple check that can save the whole download.
This time, it doesn't. So Docker contacts the registry and pulls the image down onto your machine, layer by layer.
Once the last layer arrives, the image is on your machine, ready to use. That's the pull step complete.
So what exactly did we just download? What is an image?
An image is a frozen snapshot of a filesystem, built up in layers. And it's completely inert — it can't do anything on its own. It's data, not a running process.
Each layer is one step in how the image was built: a base system at the bottom, then packages, then dependencies, and the application files on top.
Stacked together, those layers behave as a single, read-only filesystem. Add some metadata — like which program to start — and that's an image. And so far, nothing is running.
This is where create comes in. Docker takes that read-only image and lays a thin, writable layer on top of it. The image plus that new layer — together, that's a container.
And then start launches the image's main program — for nginx, that's the web server — as the first process inside the container: PID 1.
So how does this process use its filesystem? When it needs its configuration, it reads it straight from the read-only image underneath.

But when it writes something — a log file, for example — the write lands in the container's writable layer. Even modifying an existing file copies it there first. That mechanism is copy-on-write, and it's what keeps the image pristine.
Same read-only image underneath, but every change stays isolated in the container's own layer. That is a container.
So run isn't really a single operation. It's three smaller steps rolled into one convenient command.
First it pulls the image — if you don't already have it — then it creates a container from it, and finally it starts the process inside. Let's walk through each one.
So far, that's a single container sitting on top of a read-only image. But what happens when we run a second one?
Docker doesn't copy the image — and that's the smart part. It creates a second container — a new process, with its own empty writable layer — and both containers read from the same image on disk.
So when web-1 writes a log file, it lands in web-1's own layer — and only there. web-2 can't see it. One shared image underneath, completely separate changes on top.
And this is where that design really pays off. Add a third container, or a tenth — it's still one image on disk. Each new container costs only its thin, writable layer.
Now, both of these containers are running on the same kernel — the host's. So how are they kept apart? The answer is namespaces.
A namespace gives a container its own private view of just one kind of resource. There's one for process IDs, one for the network, one for filesystem mounts, and several more.
Take process IDs. Inside its own namespace, each container's main process is PID 1 — and neither container can see the other's processes at all.
And the mount namespace is what lets each container see its own stack — the shared image plus its own writable layer — as its entire root filesystem, starting at slash.
Keeping containers apart is only half the story. The other half is making sure no single container can consume all of the host's resources. That's the job of cgroups.
A cgroup — short for control group — lets you set a hard cap on how much CPU and memory a container can use out of the host's total.
We can give web-2 a tighter limit, so even at full load it can never starve web-1 — or take down the host itself.
And that's the whole picture. One shared kernel, one shared image — each container isolated by its namespaces, and constrained by its cgroups. In the end, that's all a container really is.
So, let's put the whole picture back together.
docker run nginx still looks like one simple line. But now you know everything it actually does.
Pull the image, create the container, start the process — then isolate it with namespaces, and constrain it with cgroups.
That's what happens, every time you run a container. Thanks for watching.
